label: verify-198-cal-app-4th-926
system:
You are the citation verifier in a legal-exploration pipeline. You are given one authority as cited by the pipeline's components, plus snippets showing how it was used. Using web search (prefer primary/official sources: leginfo.legislature.ca.gov, courts.ca.gov, law.justia.com, casetext.com, codes.findlaw.com, fppc.ca.gov), determine:
1. Does the authority exist?
2. Is the citation form correct (reporter cite, year, section number)?
3. Does it plausibly stand for what the snippets use it for?

Statuses: VERIFIED (exists, cite essentially right, usage consistent with what you found); MISCHARACTERIZED (exists but does not support the claimed use, or the cite attaches to the wrong case/section); NOT_FOUND (cannot locate after genuine searching); CONTESTED (real sources point in conflicting directions, or scope is genuinely unsettled); UNCLEAR (could not determine within a reasonable search).

You are NOT determining whether the authority is still good law (no Shepardizing) — say what you actually confirmed, no more. Be strict: a close-but-wrong reporter cite or a holding stretched past what sources show is MISCHARACTERIZED, not VERIFIED.
prompt:
## Authority as cited (variants seen in the documents)

- 198 Cal.App.4th 926

## How the documents used it

[delphi] …Cal.5th 372 — 3
- *Wilde v. City of Dunsmuir* (2020) 9 Cal.5th 1105 — 2

- *City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
- *Richmond v. Shasta Community Services Dist.* (2004) 32 Cal.4th 409
- *Zolly v. City of Oakland* (2022) 13 Cal.5th 858 (cited with a "verify relevance" flag)
- Gov. Code §66022

6. Verify and, if warranted, reallocate recycled-water plant debt service between base rates (systemwide benefit) and the surcharge (peak-driven benefit) based on actua…

Palmdale Water Dist.* (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926 (verify) — treated the entire $0.85 as tainted rather than severing a defensible cost-based residue. Counsel's litigation theory ("the incentive language just describes real costs") was not backed by a document that act…

## Context

The underlying question is a California public-agency hypothetical: q2 — Drought surcharge funding low-income rebates (Prop 218).

Verify this authority per your instructions.

canonical_cite.description: the correct full citation, if determinable

note.description: 1-3 sentences: what was confirmed or refuted, and the correct cite

text: {"status":"MISCHARACTERIZED","canonical_cite":"City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926","note":"The case exists and the cite form is correct, but the documents get the holding backwards. The Court of Appeal reversed judgment for the water district, invalidating its water-budget-based tiered rate structure because the district failed to show any cost-of-service basis for the tier disparities, as Proposition 218 requires proportionality to actual cost of service (conservation rationale alone was insufficient). The [dialectic] and [premortem] snippets describe budget-based tiers as 'upheld' by Palmdale — the opposite of what the court held; [delphi] merely lists the cite without characterizing it, which is fine.","source_urls":["https://www.aalrr.com/newsroom-alerts-4059","https://kmtg.com/news/legal-alerts/water-districts-new-rate-structure-failed-to-comply-with-requirements-of-proposition-218/","https://pioneerlawgroup.net/water-district-failed-show-rate-structure-complied-proposition-218-requirements-property-related-fees/"]}

data.canonical_cite: City of Palmdale v. Palmdale Water Dist. (2011) 198 Cal.App.4th 926
data.note: The case exists and the cite form is correct, but the documents get the holding backwards. The Court of Appeal reversed judgment for the water district, invalidating its water-budget-based tiered rate structure because the district failed to show any cost-of-service basis for the tier disparities, as Proposition 218 requires proportionality to actual cost of service (conservation rationale alone was insufficient). The [dialectic] and [premortem] snippets describe budget-based tiers as 'upheld' by Palmdale — the opposite of what the court held; [delphi] merely lists the cite without characterizing it, which is fine.
